entry.title: SAP Finance Certifications: Hands-On Testing in Live SAP S/4HANA Systems
entry.content:
Why SAP Finance Certifications Matter

SAP Finance certifications demonstrate your ability to not just understand concepts, but to actually execute them in a working system. Whether you're a financial or management accounting implementation consultant, these certifications prove you can deliver results from day one.

Financial Accounting Certifications

1. SAP Certified - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Financial Accounting

Learn More & Register

This certification validates your fundamental knowledge and practical skills in financial accounting processes within SAP S/4HANA. During the exam, you'll work directly in a live SAP S/4HANA system to demonstrate your ability to:

  • Process accounts payable and receivable transactions
  • Handle general ledger accounting
  • Manage asset accounting
  • Execute period-end closing activities

2. SAP Certified Associate - Implementation Consultant - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Financial Accounting

Learn More & Register

Focused on cloud implementations, this certification proves your consulting capabilities in deploying SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for financial accounting. The practical exam format ensures you can configure and implement solutions in real-world cloud scenarios.

Management Accounting Certifications

3. SAP Certified - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Management Accounting

Learn More & Register

This certification covers the controlling and management accounting aspects of SAP S/4HANA. Through hands-on exercises in a live system, you'll demonstrate proficiency in:

  • Cost center accounting
  • Internal orders
  • Product costing
  • Profitability analysis
  • Profit center accounting

4. SAP Certified Associate - Implementation Consultant - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Management Accounting

Learn More & Register

This certification prepares you to implement management accounting solutions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition environments, with practical testing that mirrors real implementation projects.

The Practical Exam Advantage

What makes these certifications truly valuable is the hands-on exam methodology. Instead of memorizing answers, you'll:

  • Work in a live SAP S/4HANA system during your exam
  • Perform actual transactions and configurations that reflect real business scenarios
  • Demonstrate problem-solving skills in a realistic environment
  • Prove you can deliver from your first day on the job

This practical approach means that when you earn your certification, employers know you have genuine, applicable skills.

Which Certification Is Right for You?

  • Choose Financial Accounting certifications if you work with general ledger, accounts payable/receivable, asset accounting, or financial reporting
  • Choose Management Accounting certifications if you focus on cost controlling, internal reporting, profitability analysis, or management decision support
  • Choose on-premise certifications for traditional SAP S/4HANA implementations
  • Choose Cloud Public Edition certifications if you're working with or transitioning to cloud-based solutions

Ready to Get Certified?

SAP Finance certifications are investments in your career that pay dividends through enhanced credibility, expanded opportunities, and demonstrated expertise. With the practical exam format, you'll gain confidence knowing that your certification reflects real-world capabilities.

Visit the links above to explore the detailed exam guides, recommended training courses, and registration information for each certification path. Your journey to becoming an SAP Certified Finance professional starts with hands-on experience and that's exactly what these certifications test!

Have you taken any of these SAP Finance certifications? Share your experience below!

entry.title: Re: VAT Codes clarification BE & ES
entry.content:
Hi,

Regarding Spain, the Tax code "AF" i s typically used when a company established in the territory of application of the Spanish VAT (TAI) renders a service to another company established in a different country. In this case, the transaction is not subject to Spanish VAT and is classified as "no sujeta al IVA por reglas de localización" (not subject due to localization rules).

You can check legal details in https://sede.agenciatributaria.gob.es/Sede/iva/iva-operaciones-comercio-exterior/prestaciones-servicios.html

Regards

Francisco.

entry.title: onSelect script: How to read dimension property value from table row and open as URL?
entry.content:
I have a table in an SAC Optimized Story. The table uses a Generic dimension called "Story" which has a property called "ExtLink" containing external URLs. I want to use the table's onSelect script to read the ExtLink value of the clicked row and open it in a new browser tab using NavigationUtils.openUrl().

I have tried using getSelections() and getResultMember(), but keep getting "Script execution failed" errors at runtime. What is the correct way to access a dimension property value from a selected table row in the onSelect script?

Thank you for any advice!

 

var selections = PubAssets_Table.getSelections();
if (selections.length > 0) {
    var member = PubAssets_Table.getDataSource().getResultMember("Story", selections[0]);
    var url = member.properties["ExtLink"];
    if (url && url.startsWith("http")) {
        NavigationUtils.openUrl(url, true);
    }
}

If you clicked on the hyperlink cell, I wanted to open up a new browser to the destination of the URL.

entry.title: Custom BSEG Field Filled via OBBH Substitution but Not Visible in FBL1N ALV
entry.content:
Question
I added a custom field to table BSEG via append structure:


ZZCUSTOMFIELD (CHAR20)

The table was activated successfully.

Then I configured a substitution in OBBH:

Application Area: FI

Callup Point: 2 (Line Item)

Company Code: 1000

Substitution: ZFI_BK

Inside the substitution:

Created a step

Assigned field: BSEG-ZZCUSTOMFIELD

Substitution type: Constant

Value: 'LIBRARY'

Prerequisite: TRUE

Substitution was generated successfully.

Testing
Posted a new vendor document.

Checked BSEG via SE16N → field is correctly populated.

Extended the ALV layout in FBL1N to include ZZCUSTOMFIELD.

Issue
Although:

The field exists in BSEG

The substitution fills the field correctly

The value is stored in the database

The value does not appear in FBL1N ALV output, even after adding the field to the layout.

Question
Is there any additional configuration required to make an append field from BSEG visible in FBL1N?

Does FBL1N require:

Logical database enhancement?

Structure enhancement (e.g., RFPOSXEXT)?

BTE / implicit enhancement?

Field catalog adjustment?

What is the correct approach to display a custom BSEG append field in FBL1N?

entry.title: Batch determination based on WBS BOM item characteristics in CO27
entry.content:
I have materials with the same material number that represent either a whole unit or a rest piece.  A rest piece is a by-product from a production order. Each rest piece will get a separate batch number with a specific batch characteristic value and will be stored in an IM storage location as a restricted use batch to prevent taken into account for MRP calculation; we can't use MRP areas as most of our stock is project stock. For a next production order where I want to consume this particular rest piece I create a WBS BOM (each production order is based on a WBS BOM) where I have item lines for the whole units (item cat L, EWM managed)  and  separate lines for the rest pieces (item cat R, IM managed) and in the line for the item cat R I assign characteristics values to identify that particular rest piece. For each rest piece an individual line in my WBS BOM with specific characteristics value

I can't do automatic batch determination in the production order, because the batches for the whole pieces are entered manually based what was on top of the stack during picking and also SAP doesn't support the reading of batch characteristics from WBS BOM. for batch determination.

So for the rest piece I start the picking process in CO27. 

I have maintained the whole logic for batch determination for IM based on plant / mvt (261 Q) and it works, but it gives me more results when I have multiple rest pieces because it doesn't consider the value in the component line characteristic

Question: How can I get the specific characteristics value(s)  from the WBS BOM item classification (also in the reservation for the item) for the rest piece into my selection, so I only get that 1 particular batch as result?

entry.title: SAP DRC Croatia
entry.content:
Hello experts,

I am currently working on the implementation of incoming supplier eInvoices for Croatia using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC), cloud edition and would like to understand the end-to-end setup and network registration process.

🔹 Business Scenario

We want to receive supplier electronic invoices in our SAP backend system via SAP DRC.

🔹 My Questions

  1. Participant registration in the network

    • How is our company OIB registered in the eDelivery/PEPPOL network when using SAP DRC?

    • Is the registration in SMP/SML handled automatically by SAP DRC, or must it be done externally?

  2. Role of intermediary (FINA)

    • Is it mandatory to use FINA as an intermediary/access point for Croatia when receiving supplier invoices?

    • If yes:

      • What are the high-level steps to integrate FINA with SAP DRC?

      • Do we need to provide our OIB and certificates to FINA for SMP registration?

      • How is the routing from FINA to SAP DRC technically achieved?

  3. Certificates and communication setup

    • Which certificates are required specifically for inbound document flow?

    • Is the P12 certificate issued by FINA used for:

      • authentication,

      • AS4 communication,

      • or SMP registration?

  4. DRC configuration

    • Which configuration in DRC determines that inbound supplier invoices for our OIB are routed to our SAP business system?

    • Are there any Croatia-specific document types or communication arrangements required?

🔹 Current Understanding (please correct if wrong)

  • SAP DRC acts as the receiving endpoint and forwards the documents to the business system.

  • The company must be discoverable in the network via its OIB.

  • SMP/SML registration is required for inbound document routing.

    Your support would really help us deliver this, thanks in advance 🙂

To achieve this, I need to create comprehensive guides covering key areas such as:- test preparation, test execution and defect management.  I plan to start building these guides using resources available online and in the SAP Help Portal. However, if anyone in the community has already created similar documentation or guides that I could reuse (with minor modifications) or draw inspiration from, it would be incredibly helpful and save a lot of time. 
Thank you in advance for your support! 
SAP Cloud ALM

entry.title: Introducing Tags in Our HCM Learning Group
entry.content:
To help everyone get faster, more accurate responses, we’re introducing SAP Managed tags for all new posts.

Why SAP Managed tags Matter

SAP Managed tags help organize questions so the right people see them quickly.

When posts are not tagged, they can:

  •            Get overlooked
  •            Take longer to reach the right experts
  •            Receive delayed responses

In short: No SAP Managed tag = slower answers.

Using the correct tag ensures your question or comment gets in front of the right audience as quickly as possible.

✅ How to Use SAP Managed Tags

When creating a post, simply select an SAP Managed tag from a list of tags below that best matches your question or comment. If more than one applies, choose the most relevant one.

Here is a list of SAP Managed tags to use:

#SAP SuccessFactors Platform

#SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central

#SAP Performance and Goals

#SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting

#SAP SuccessFactors Succession

#SAP SuccessFactors Compensation and Variable

#SAP SuccessFactors Learning

#SAP SuccessFactors Reporting Solutions

#SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding

#SAP SuccessFactors Career and Development Planning

#SmartRecruiters for SAP SuccessFactors

#Cross Product Topic

@user’s name

❓ Learning Product Related Topic

Choose an SAP Managed tag from the list above if your post is related to one of the learning products listed.

Examples: #SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central

“Is the new People Profile optional?”

“Where do I find the RBP framework?”

🔧 Technical Issue

Use this tag if you are experience an error or you have a general topic to discuss.

Examples: #Cross Product Topic

“Do I use a # or @ when posting?.”

“My account settings won’t save.”

When selecting a label in the Labels field, you can quickly identify the most frequently used options because the common labels are highlighted in blue. This visual cue helps you choose the appropriate label more efficiently and ensures consistency across entries.

To assign a label, simply click on the field and select one of the available options from the list. If you are unsure which label to use, refer to the highlighted (blue) labels first, as they represent the most commonly applied categories.

The ‘Message User Tag’ is an optional tag. You may use it to add additional context or internal notes related to the entry, but it is not required. If no extra clarification is needed, you can leave this field blank.

🎯 @MichaelJordan

Use this tag to reach a user directly. Please note that this tag is not recommended for technical and product topics as a response might be delayed.

Quick Reminder

Before posting, remember to do the following:

  • Choose the SAP Managed tag that best fits your topic.
  • Make your subject line clear and specific.
  • Provide enough details in the body for others to help you efficiently.
  • Select a label

Thank you for helping us keep our community efficient and easy to navigate!

entry.title: Re: Improving the SAP Community together, one comment at a time
entry.content:
@gyoung thanks for a great comment. I do take issue with your phrasing "relying on their customers (?) to support internal moderating capacity". The SAP community has always been about, for, and owned by the community. Not customers specifically. Not partners. Not any group in particular. And a community of this scale, like other communities, must almost by definition be based on collective efforts. 

I do salute you for the well structured and well thought out content of your comment, unfortunately the people like you and the rest of the folks involved in the comments to this post, people who care, are in the minority - or so it seems to me (as always, and as stated in my bio online (such as at https://www.linkedin.com/in/djadams/), opinions online like this are all my own).

I think trying to curate accurate content here on this platform for LLMs is an impossible task. The problem is far bigger than any given platform, and the platforms themselves, and even the communities that exist on the platforms, cannot be tasked with solving that problem.

entry.title: Exchange Rate Differences Not Generated in Automatic Payments
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I am working with SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and I have encountered a behavior that I do not fully understand regarding the posting of exchange rate differences during the automatic payment run.

Here is the scenario:

  • The supplier invoice is posted in USD on the 18th.

  • The payment is executed in USD on the 20th through the Automatic Payment process.

  • The payment method used is configured as cross‑border and is linked to a USD bank account.

  • However, in the journal entry generated by the automatic payment, no exchange rate difference is posted, even though the payment date is different from the invoice posting date.

If I perform the payment manually, SAP does recalculate the exchange rate on the payment date and correctly posts the exchange rate difference in the journal entry.

My question is: Is there any way in SAP Public Cloud to make the Automatic Payment Run post exchange rate differences when the payment date differs from the invoice date? In other words, is there any configuration that allows the automatic payment process to recalculate the exchange rate or post the realized exchange rate difference?

Any guidance or experience on this topic would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Declaração de Imposto de Renda na Fonte para o ano base 2025 – Entidades Públicas
entry.content:
Olá pessoal, 

Para atender ao Ato Declaratório Executivo Nº 6/2026 que implementa mudanças no layout do relatório DIRF (HBRDIRF1), foi disponibilizada a SAP Note 3715413 – LC BR – DIRF base year 2025 for public entities.  

Essa alteração é essencial para Entidades Públicas que podem declarar as informações fiscais de 2025 através da DIRF. Para garantir conformidade e evitar complicações, é fundamental implementar essa mudança conforme descrito na SAP Note. 

Essa alteração adapta o formato do relatório as novas exigências para o ano-base de 2025:  

Novo Valor de Campo: O campo "Ident. layout" foi atualizado para o valor "F4Q51M4" no arquivo gerado pelo DIRF (HBRDIRF1). 

Para detalhes adicionais, consulte a referida SAP Note. 

Gostou desse post? 

Dê um Like e compartilhe o conteúdo com seus colegas. 

Fiquem à vontade para deixar um feedback, comentário ou pergunta sobre este Blog post no espaço abaixo. E não esqueçam de seguir a tag HCM Payroll Brazil na SAP Community para ficarem ligados nas últimas notícias. 

Um abraço, 
Juliano Klein 
Time de HCM Brasil

entry.title: Re: How to integrate public cloud ERP with standalone EWM？
entry.content: I’d use standard EWM integration APIs and set up separate communication arrangements for each system. Each EWM can connect through its own scope items and queues without blocking the others.

entry.title: Re: Issue with AI160 JavaScript Exercise – /api/agent/tri...
entry.content:
@gregorw  Curl works perfectly.. only SAP AI Core deployment doesn't support /chat/completionsendpoint.

curl -v -i -X POST \
  "https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com/v2/inference/deployments/d48a78b0811caa3b/chat/completions" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: <123>" \
  -H "X-Tenant: e125da77-03b4-4f88-ad8c-d5055c2cfaea" \
  -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello!"}],"max_tokens":200}'

When running the agent code (exercises/javascript/app/agent.ts or po-agents.js), I encounter this TypeScript compilation error:

Argument of type 'BaseMessage<MessageStructure<MessageToolSet>, MessageType>[]' is not assignable to parameter of type 'BaseLanguageModelInput'. Type 'BaseMessage<MessageStructure<MessageToolSet>, MessageType>[]' is not assignable to type 'BaseMessageLike[]'. [... full error in comment below]

Error location:

async function callModel({ messages }: typeof MessagesAnnotation.State) { const response = await modelWithTools.invoke(messages); // ❌ Error here return { messages: [response] }; }

Happy to provide more details or test fixes! Would love to see this resolved for productive SAP AI Core usage.

Thanks for the great work!
Manoj

Graph failure: target1 failed with the following error: Communication with DS backend was unexpectedly closed DBS-070401: Dataflow DF_cd87c0cbaf9f4ec4a54c4d8954b0d9ea_1|DT__1f96ad9674818_1_308 ODBC data source <97030a11-77ef-4d9f-af1d-9588c9d5ec3f.hana.prod-in30.hanacloud.ondemand.com> error message for operation <SQLExecute>: <[SAP AG][LIBODBCHDB SO][HDBODBC] General error;287 cannot insert NULL or update to NULL: Not nullable "GDATU" column>. ; group=default, message=Graph failure: target1 failed with the following error: Communication with DS backend was unexpectedly closed DBS-070401: Dataflow DF_cd87c0cbaf9f4ec4a54c4d8954b0d9ea_1|DT__1f96ad9674818_1_308 ODBC data source <97030a11-77ef-4d9f-af1d-9588c9d5ec3f.hana.prod-in30.hanacloud.ondemand.com> error message for operation <SQLExecute>: <[SAP AG][LIBODBCHDB SO][HDBODBC] General error;287 cannot insert NULL or update to NULL: Not nullable "GDATU" column>."

 

Have attached the error screenshot.

Can someone throw some light on this issues.

entry.title: Re: Sales price condition in Customer Projects for Subcon...
entry.content:
Why You See Different Results

Access Sequence with WBS + Personal Number
When you maintain the sales price condition with this combination, the system doesn’t find it during revenue recognition. Instead, it falls back to the subcontracting PO price (80€). That’s why your planned revenue is overridden by the procurement rate.

Access Sequence with Project + Personal Number
Here, the system finds the condition record directly tied to the project. Because the project condition is prioritized in the access sequence, the sales price (100€) is applied correctly during revenue recognition, even though the PO is 80€.

Root Cause
Revenue recognition in customer projects is driven by condition determination logic. The system checks condition records in the order defined by the access sequence. If the first valid condition is not found, it moves down the list.

  • In your first setup, the WBS + personal number condition wasn’t being picked up (likely due to missing linkage in the timesheet → WBS assignment).
  • In the second setup, the project + personal number condition matched directly, so the system applied the correct sales rate.

entry.title: change key fields in custom BO in SAP C4C V1 after transported to other system
entry.content:
Hi All,

I have a custom BO as follows

import AP.Common.GDT as apCommonGDT;

businessobject CBO {

        // Root node must not be specified, it is created implicitly
        //
        // Add elements here. 
        // 
        // To declare an element as alternative key, use the [AlternativeKey] annotation.
        // Please note:
        // - An alternative key of type UUID exists implicitly for each node, enabling the node
        //   to be used as an association target.
        // - The QueryByElements query is created automatically for each node.
        // - Code completion is available with CTRL + SPACE.
		
   [Label("field1")] [AlternativeKey] element SerialNumber : ID ;
   [Label ("field2")]  [AlternativeKey] element field2 :LANGUAGEINDEPENDENT_MEDIUM_Description;
   [Label ("field3")]    element field3 :LANGUAGEINDEPENDENT_MEDIUM_Description;
   [Label ("field4")]   element field4 :LANGUAGEINDEPENDENT_MEDIUM_Description; 
   
   }

This CBO is transported to various tenants. I want to change the CBO as follows in which I need to change the annotation for one of the fields and make it as non alternativekey. Does this need a deletion patch? Is this allowed using the regular patch movement. 

import AP.Common.GDT as apCommonGDT;

businessobject CBO {

        // Root node must not be specified, it is created implicitly
        //
        // Add elements here. 
        // 
        // To declare an element as alternative key, use the [AlternativeKey] annotation.
        // Please note:
        // - An alternative key of type UUID exists implicitly for each node, enabling the node
        //   to be used as an association target.
        // - The QueryByElements query is created automatically for each node.
        // - Code completion is available with CTRL + SPACE.
		
   [Label("field1")] [AlternativeKey] element SerialNumber : ID ;
   [Label ("field2")]  element field2 :LANGUAGEINDEPENDENT_MEDIUM_Description;
   [Label ("field3")]    element field3 :LANGUAGEINDEPENDENT_MEDIUM_Description;
   [Label ("field4")]   element field4 :LANGUAGEINDEPENDENT_MEDIUM_Description; 
   
   }

  Let me know if anyone has encountered this situation before.

Best Regards

Gauri Nargunde

entry.title: Re: mm02 attachments in smartforms
entry.content:
Hi everyone — thanks for the discussion. Let me clarify a few points for the OP and close the loop technically.

The code snippet I shared was based on patterns I’ve used in older ECC on‑premise systems where customers had custom GOS‑related function modules in the SAP namespace.
Different landscapes vary a lot — especially in utilities and manufacturing — so it’s possible that some of these modules don’t exist in newer S/4HANA releases or in other customer systems.

For the OP’s requirement (displaying MM02 attachments in SmartForms), the general technical approach remains valid:

  • Read GOS attachments for the material
  • Extract the binary
  • Convert to a SmartForms‑compatible format (BMP/TIFF)
  • Pass the image to the form

The exact APIs differ by release, so the recommended modern alternatives are:

  1. CL_GOS_MANAGER / CL_GOS_DOCUMENT_SERVICE
  2. SCMS_BINARY_TO_XSTRING
  3. SCMS_XSTRING_TO_BINARY
  4. Storing the converted image temporarily in SE78 if SmartForms requires BMP/TIFF

If you’re on S/4HANA, the DMS‑based approach shared by @Joery  is also a solid option. Happy to help refine the solution depending on your system version.

entry.title: Re: onSelect script: How to read dimension property value...
entry.content:
Hello @Hyun_Lee1 ,

 

I tried recreating your setup, and for reasons that I can't figure out, the properties object of ResultMember is empty, no matter how I code it. 
Your script fails in line 4, because you try to access ["ExtUrl"] of undefined - which causes a crash. 

There is another way - which is to use ResultSet:

var PubAssets_Table = Table_1;

var selections = PubAssets_Table.getSelections();
console.log("Selections");
console.log(selections);

if (selections.length > 0) {
    //Get ResultSet 
	var member = PubAssets_Table.getDataSource().getResultSet(selections[0],0,1);
	console.log("Member:");
	console.log(member);
	
	//Get Properties:
	//NOTE: The property MUST be in the table
	var properties = member[0]["Story"].properties;
	console.log("Properties");
	console.log(properties);
	
	//Get URL:
	var url = properties["Story.URL"];	
	
	//Open Link
	if (url && url.startsWith("http")) {
			//NavigationUtils.openUrl("https://www.google.com/", true); //<-- For external websites, fully quality the URL.
			NavigationUtils.openUrl(url, true);
		}
}

Which will give you the following output:

You can then pass the URL 

//Get URL:
var url = properties["Story.URL"];	

//Open Link
if (url && url.startsWith("http")) {
//NavigationUtils.openUrl("https://www.google.com/", true); //<-- For external websites, fully quality the URL.
			NavigationUtils.openUrl(url, true);
		}
}

Which should do the trick. 

Note: This solution requires you to add the property to the table. If ExtUrl is not a part of the table, properties will be empty.

Hope this helps 🙂

entry.title: Designing Shopfloor Experiences Faster with POD 2.0 and AI-Assisted Creation
entry.content:
With the upcoming release, SAP Digital Manufacturing plans General Availability of POD 2.0 in DM Rel. 2605 (subject to change!), introducing a significant step forward in how Production Operator Dashboards (PODs) are designed, adapted, and experienced on the shop floor. POD 2.0 represents more than a technical enhancement. It expands the design paradigm from configuration-centric UI setup to flexible experience creation tailored to real production processes.

What’s New in POD 2.0

The new generation of POD design brings powerful capabilities that increase flexibility, usability, and performance:

  • WYSIWYG Designer for visual layout creation
  • More granular plugins for modular composition
  • Optimized performance for faster loading and execution
  • Flexible page and dialog design
  • Precise placement of UI elements such as buttons, texts, images, and links directly on plugins
  • Benefit from a comprehensive POD context and access more data in an easy way

Together, these innovations allow manufacturing organizations to design interfaces that align closely with operational reality instead of relying on fixed screen structures.

Tailoring PODs to Individual Business Needs

One of the key strengths of POD 2.0 is the ability to design customer-specific dialogs and pages with minimal effort. This enables organizations to reflect their own processes, terminology, and interaction patterns directly in execution interfaces.

For example, customers can:

  • Create dialogs tailored to internal workflows
  • Integrate production process logic into screens
  • Guide workers with process-specific pages
  • Combine standard plugins with custom extensions
  • Apply corporate design standards to shopfloor applications

This flexibility allows manufacturing teams to design execution environments that match their processes, rather than adjusting processes to match the system. With the enhanced capabilities of POD 2.0, many scenarios that previously required custom development or extensions can now be implemented through configuration alone, which can significantly accelerate project timelines; in fact, certain custom plugins can now be realized directly using standard POD 2.0 design features.

Fun fact: even the example POD page shown here was generated with the help of AI to save time during design.

 

Enabling AI-Assisted POD Creation

A particularly exciting aspect of POD 2.0 lies in its technical foundation. Each POD page, plugin configuration, dialog, and layout definition is represented as structured JSON. This structured format can be understood as a design language describing how a POD behaves and appears. Because of this standardized representation, it becomes possible to enable generative AI models such as large language models to learn this structure and automatically create POD configurations from natural-language descriptions.

Instead of manually assembling screens step by step, users could describe what they want, for example:

“Create a POD page for packaging with operator inputs and confirmation dialog.”

An AI system can then generate the corresponding configuration structure. How to enable this in practice? A LLM can be configured in a flexible way depending on the use case — ranging from lightweight setups to more advanced configurations. In simple scenarios, providing representative JSON snippets for POD pages, dialogs, and plugins, along with clear instructions and design conventions, can already allow the model to generate valid layouts. In more advanced scenarios, organizations can supply structured rule sets, templates, and extended configuration context to support complex requirements such as standardized rollout patterns or even legacy UI migration projects. This scalability allows teams to choose the level of enablement that best fits their needs while maintaining consistency and governance. 

At this point, a common question is: how do you actually obtain the JSON structure behind POD 2.0 and how can it be provided to an LLM? In practice, this is straightforward. Within the "Manage POD 2.0" app, pages, dialogs, and even individual plugins can be copied directly to the clipboard. This means the underlying JSON definition is instantly available and can be supplied as a code snippet to a configured LLM. From there, business context can be added either directly within the snippet or through model-specific instructions. Once the model understands this structure and context, it can reliably generate new POD elements that follow the same syntax, logic, and design conventions.

The following snippet shows an example element definition for a numeric operator input field (you find this as 'Input' as a plugin in Manage POD 2.0 app) including its widget type, basic properties, and optional value binding that can be used when the entered data should be stored for downstream process logic (e.g. in order to hand it over to a production process as input). In practice, multiple element definitions like this can be provided to the model, enabling it to learn a broader set of UI building blocks and generate more complex, context-aware POD layouts.

With this structure and context in place, the configured model can now interpret functional requirements and translate them into concrete POD designs. The example below illustrates how a simple natural-language request is transformed into a ready-to-use configuration.

 

AI generated JSON can be copied easily into the Manage POD 2.0 App:

Why This Matters

AI-assisted POD generation can provide value across roles:

Partners and consultants

  • Rapidly build tailored demos
  • Showcase customer scenarios with minimal preparation
  • Prototype layouts during workshops

Implementers

  • Reduce manual design effort
  • Accelerate delivery timelines
  • Iterate more efficiently

Customers

  • Adapt screens faster to operational needs
  • Maintain greater flexibility
  • Extend standard functionality with their own design logic

This approach shifts POD design from manual configuration toward description-driven generation.

To make this more tangible, consider a practical scenario: a customer or partner starts with a manufacturing process that defines certain requirements for operator guidance and the corresponding UI. Instead of translating this process into a detailed technical specification and then manually designing a POD, the process can simply be described in natural language. A properly configured LLM can interpret whether the request represents a simple data-entry screen or a guided process. In the example above, the requirement is limited to entering values such as batch and potency. Based on these inputs, the process can continue automatically by triggering a production process—for example, for quantity calculation. The same configured model can also generate UI elements such as buttons and directly link them to production process logic. If a customer has a multi-step workflow, for instance a pizza production scenario with steps like dough preparation, adding toppings, and baking, the model can automatically generate multiple pages, including optional e-signatures for compliance. Progress across steps can be captured via data collections, supporting full traceability of execution. These records can in turn be tied to signatures (via the signature reason) or process logic, providing a strong foundation for compliance scenarios such as GMP documentation.

The following example demonstrates how a LLM can translate a pure business process into a POD Design:

 

 

 

All pages were created using a specially configured LLM

 

It is important to note that approaches such as implementing worker guidance and execution tracking via data collection currently represent an implementation or consulting approach rather than a native architectural principle; however, this method is highly effective in many real-world scenarios and is widely used to support documentation and compliance requirements.

Logging of e-signatures with corresponding steps

Clear log of worker guidance based on Data Collection

Strategic Direction: Clear Separation of UI and Process Logic

Strategically, SAP Digital Manufacturing is evolving toward a model in which the POD primarily serves as the UI design layer, while business and process logic is orchestrated in the Production Process Designer (PPD). This separation is essential for scalability, as it avoids the need to create and maintain large numbers of POD variants for different products, scenarios, or process variations. As part of the vision and future roadmap, POD 2.0 is planned to be increasingly driven by the production process engine, enabling plugins to function as flexible “canvas” UI components that are dynamically populated with business context at runtime. This requires extended interaction between production processes and PODs so that processes can call specific plugins, pages, or dialogs with parameters, while UI elements render content dynamically based on process definitions.

Taken together, this points toward a broader long term vision for the future of user interfaces: the UI becomes the canvas, business logic the film, and AI the projector—dynamically displaying the right content, guidance, and interaction exactly when needed, instead of relying on static screen designs. SAP has already embedded AI capabilities in SAP Digital Manufacturing, including AI-assisted production engineering, and plans to continuously expand AI across the solution landscape, potentially extending its use directly into POD design 

Unlocking Innovation Across the Ecosystem

The key opportunity is not a specific AI solution, but the openness of the concept. Because POD definitions are structured and machine-readable as they are JSONs, different AI technologies can be used to generate them. This creates opportunities for partners, customers, and developers to build their own accelerators, assistants, or generators tailored to their requirements. Such openness encourages experimentation and innovation across the ecosystem.

Looking Ahead

With POD 2.0, SAP Digital Manufacturing moves toward a future where shopfloor user experiences can be created faster, adapted more easily, and aligned more closely with real production needs. The combination of flexible design architecture and the possibility of AI-assisted generation introduces a new level of efficiency and creativity in manufacturing interface design.

entry.title: Re: Trying to reconcile CO09 with BAPI_MATERIAL_AVAILABILITY
entry.content:
Hi all — just to clarify the concern about whether my answer was AI‑generated: it wasn’t. The explanation I shared came from my own project experience with CO09, batch‑segment ATP, and BAPI_MATERIAL_AVAILABILITY.

The arithmetic slip was simply a typing mistake while explaining the ATP logic — not the output of a tool. Anyone who has worked with CO09 knows that reconciling ATP requires looking at the correct segment (batch‑level vs. plant‑level), and that was the intent of my explanation.

I fully respect the SAP Community guidelines on GenAI transparency and follow them when I actually use a tool. In this case, the answer was written manually.

If the accepted answer is causing moderation challenges, I’m happy for the OP to unaccept it so the moderators can take whatever action they feel is appropriate. My goal was only to help the OP understand how the BAPI aligns with CO09.

entry.title: Stay on top of regulatory changes in SAP Business Network (and more...)
entry.content:
For SAP customers who want to keep an eye on how the products they use adapt to regulatory changes, the SAP Regulatory Change Manager is an easy tool to use and explore. As e-invoicing regulations keep evolving around the globe, the Regulatory Change Manager is now accessible to all SAP Business Network trading partners, so they can gain information on the mandate support per country for SAP Business Network. 

Using the app is pretty straightforward: 

  1. Go to https://app.main.regulatory-change-manager.cloud.sap/,
  2. Navigate to the All Regulatory Changes tab,
  3. Select "SAP Business Network for Procurement" as product, and choose the country/countries relevant to you,
  4. Click the "Go" button,
  5. Click on the result line for accessing information.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Signing up as user

For security reasons you need to be logged in with an SAP ID to access the tool. Please use the username and password you're using on sap.com* (if you have one) or create one in a minute by following 3 easy steps. 

Having an SAP ID allows you to save your filters and to subscribe to news and update on your product of interest. 

                    

 

We invite you to pay the Regulatory Change Manager a visit on a regular basis to read how SAP supports upcoming regulations, especially as e-invoicing and other legal mandates keep evolving around the world. 

 

* Please note these credentials are NOT related to the credentials you use to log in to your SAP Business Network supplier account. 

Looking for more resources for suppliers on SAP Business Network? Visit our dedicated topic page to be kept up to date.

entry.title: Re: Exchange Rate Differences Not Generated in Automatic...
entry.content:
Dear @Jessicaaguilar1,

What you’re seeing is a known/expected behavior when a specific setting is active for the paying company code in the Automatic Payment process Manage Automatic Payments app F0770. In Manage Automatic Payments, SAP can be configured to take the local-currency amount from the cleared open item instead of translating the payment again on the payment posting date. In that case no realized FX gain/loss line is generated, even if the payment date differs from the invoice posting date. This is described in SAP Note for Public Edition

SAP Notes below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002652016

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002861801

The key configuration:

Go to CBC ID 101001 Set Up Paying Company Codes for Payment Transactions and check whether the indicator No Exchange Rate Difference is set for your paying company code/payment setup

 

  • If that indicator is set, then the automatic payment run will not post realized FX differences it uses the invoice translation
  • If you want realized FX differences at payment posting time, make sure this indicator is NOT set then rerun the proposal/payment

as described in the SAP Notes above

Prerequisite FX:

Once FX differences are allowed, the system must be able to determine the accounts for posting them transaction key KDF etc. SAP points to maintaining this in Public Cloud configuration Automatic Account Determination / Define Accounts for Exchange Rate Differences

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002857528

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP Global Healthcare Industry Strategy: A Modular, Data-Driven, Open, and Interoperable Platform
entry.content:
The healthcare industry is at an industry inflection point: Rising patient expectations, persistent margin pressure, workforce shortages, evolving reimbursement, escalating cyber threats, and a wave of technological advances are reshaping how providers operate. In such conditions, one-size-fits-all, monolithic systems cannot keep pace. The organizations that will thrive are building modular, cloud-based platforms where integrated applications, context-rich data, and meaningful AI reinforce each other in a continuous flywheel, running efficiently today while adapting quickly to whatever tomorrow brings.

Across the sector, four realities now define the agenda:

  1. AI accelerates and touches nearly everything;
  2. business transformation never stops and needs to prove its value continuously;
  3. patient journeys rarely begin or end within a single entity;
  4. context-rich insights are essential for better, faster decisions.

The task is to master all of these, not sequentially but simultaneously, and to be prepared for the ones you cannot yet see.

Why healthcare providers must keep adjusting

Across markets, the economics of care are shifting as costs rise and reimbursement models evolve, forcing providers to find new efficiencies and transparency without compromising care quality. At the same time, many clinical and administrative teams are stretched thin. Reducing and automating low-value work and streamlining processes has become essential to return time to care and improve staff experience. Patients increasingly move across networks and settings; their journeys do not begin or end within a single entity, and they expect their information to follow them securely and seamlessly. Consolidation and the formation of clinical networks add further complexity, as hospital groups must operate multi-site, multi-vendor landscapes without locking in. Regulators meanwhile demand stronger privacy, security, and auditability. In addition, providers are expected to contribute to research and innovation, which requires governed, high-quality data that can be shared responsibly. In this context, change is not a project with an end date - it is a continuous capability.

Why modular, data-driven technology wins

The technological response to these realities is clear: Monoliths are giving way to composable, cloud-based platforms where capabilities can evolve independently and safely. Interoperability is a first principle: open standards make it feasible to plug and play with electronic medical record and revenue cycle systems, devices, and third-party solutions. A data-fabric architecture becomes the backbone, unifying, governing, and productizing data across silos for operations, analytics, and research. AI then moves from hype to impact when it is responsibly embedded in processes, grounded in high-quality, context-rich, governed data and managed with explainability and controls. Security and sovereignty must be designed from the beginning to keep sensitive health data under the right jurisdiction, up to and including deployment on a sovereign cloud in and for a specific country. Because healthcare runs in mixed environments, the architecture must be hybrid by design, bridging on-premises and cloud systems while preserving control. Extensibility also matters: Open APIs and both no-code, low-code, and pro-code tools featured by AI coding toolchains allow organizations to adapt quickly to new regulations, clinical workflows, and innovations without compromising the clean core that keeps your systems stable over time and allows future innovations.

How SAP contributes: a digital health platform you can compose

We at SAP recognize this and, grounded in realities at customer sites, have defined our SAP global healthcare industry strategy accordingly: To provide our current and future customers with a hybrid, modular healthcare system landscape that runs intelligent and automated processes on SAP together with solutions from our partner ecosystem, so providers can deliver better outcomes for every patient, every time.

Interoperability in an open ecosystem environment is foundational: We embrace open healthcare standards, including HL7 FHIR® through SAP Health Data Services for FHIR on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), so providers can integrate clinical and operational landscapes at scale. We are open to AnyEMR and MultiEMR, meaning you can choose the electronic medical record (EMR) systems that fit your needs, even operate multiple EMRs in parallel across a hospital group. The same openness applies to revenue cycle management (RCM): AnyRCM and MultiRCM are supported, so you can run the billing and patient-accounting systems that work best for your context and locale. While SAP does not develop or market Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) or EMR/RCM systems by ourselves, we rely on a broad partner ecosystem to extend clinical capabilities, giving customers freedom of choice and accelerating innovation. You can explore healthcare extensions on SAP Store, track progress here via SAP Healthcare Community updates and SAP News, and follow co-innovation signals through Sapphire Ventures.

A clean, modern core for business operations remains essential: SAP Cloud ERP (SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public or private edition) provides best-in-class capabilities for finance, spend management, and supply chain, forming the backbone of efficient, resilient hospital operations. You can compose end-to-end solutions by combining these capabilities with industry-specific partner solutions for hospital and clinical needs (for example, Hospital Information Systems (HIS) and Clinical Information Systems (CIS)). SAP is also verticalizing core business capabilities for healthcare and publishing industry roadmaps to provide transparency and alignment with regulatory and clinical requirements.

Data is the connective tissue: SAP Business Data Cloud implements a healthcare-ready data fabric, enabling you to access, combine, and govern context-rich data across applications and vendors with lineage and policy enforcement. It is being expanded with healthcare-specific semantics, interoperability accelerators, and governance templates for consent, retention, and de-identification, so providers can package data safely and at speed. This is how you move from siloed data to “Patient 360", blending clinical, operational, and financial signals to coordinate care, optimize throughput, and improve outcomes. It is also how you provide research-ready data products with the required quality, confidentiality, and anonymization for internal and external studies, within and across institutions, supporting the future data economy.

For a deeper dive on treating clinical and operational data as a governed organizational asset and what that implies for ownership, quality, stewardship, and value realization, see Data as medicine’s backbone: redefining its value to foster innovation in the data economy | BMJ Health & Care Informatics

AI then becomes meaningful: It begins with embedded intelligence and productive assistance, and culminates in agentic capabilities: SAP Business AI infuses intelligence across the entire SAP solution portfolio, while SAP Joule, our extensible assistant, empowers teams work smarter with enterprise context at their fingertips. With SAP Joule Studio, organizations can create governed, customer-specific AI scenarios that leverage SAP-provided access to leading foundation models, grounded in your data and subject to robust controls for safety, privacy, and auditability. Together, these layers establish the foundation on which the next evolution - agentic AI - can operate autonomously and responsibly.

All of this is orchestrated on SAP BTP, which provides services for application, process, and data integrations, APIs and eventing, including prebuild integration content, workflow, security, and both low-code/no-code and pro-code tools, all embedded in AI, you need to extend, automate, integrate, and innovate without compromising the clean core of your systems. Because sovereignty matters, you can deploy on sovereign-cloud options to meet strict residency, control, and regulatory requirements. Efficient operations and sustained transformation are anchored by SAP Services & Support and SAP Learning, complemented by experienced partners who bring proven healthcare accelerators.

Modernize at your pace

Every organization is at a different starting point. For providers adopting SAP Cloud ERP for the first time, SAP GROW accelerates deployment with best practices and fast time-to-value. For those transitioning from legacy ERP, RISE with SAP provides a guided path to SAP Cloud ERP with transformation services, tailored to guide organizations through every phase of their digital transformation journey. Many hospitals still run SAP Business Suite 7 (ECC), whose mainstream maintenance ends in 2027. To provide runway, SAP offers extended maintenance through 2030 and a Transition Option in SAP Private Cloud through 2033, so you can plan and execute your migration deliberately and de-risked. Here, most providers favor separating ERP and HIS transformations, with ERP first, to harmonize master data and structures and reduce risk when integrating clinical systems.

What does this mean for you, our customers

For leadership teams

Standardize the core while keeping the edge flexible. Harmonize finance, procurement, and supply chain on a clean core, and integrate AnyEMR/AnyRCM to reflect clinical realities at each site. The result is greater transparency into cost drivers, improved resilience in sourcing and inventory, and a lower total cost of ownership as cloud operations and a clean core reduce technical debt and simplify upgrades.

For clinicians and care teams

Interoperable platforms reduce friction. When the right data flows to the point of care through standards-based integration, documentation becomes lighter, handoffs become safer, and decisions are supported by timely, context-rich insights.  AI becomes a practical and personal assistant rather than a novelty, helping prepare notes, highlight risks, predict discharge readiness where appropriate, and increasingly supporting activities autonomously - always grounded in governed data. This relieves staff from administrative burden and frees up time for higher‑value, patient‑focused work.

For research and innovation leaders

A governed data fabric creates a trustworthy pipeline for studies and collaborations. You can publish well-defined data products with privacy and provenance protected by policy, enabling partnerships with universities, life sciences, and public health without reinventing the wheel each time.

For hospital groups and networks

Open, modular architectures enable multi-site agility. Organizations can operate MultiEMR/MultiRCM across entities, harmonize where it counts, and maintain local autonomy where needed, avoiding lock-in while still gaining economies of scale.

For IT, security, and compliance teams

Unified governance brings consistency. Policies apply across applications and data, controls are auditable, and residency requirements can be met with sovereign-cloud deployments when needed.

These are not theoretical benefits: Hirslanden, for example, modernized core processes and systems to support new clinical practices, regulatory standards, and cost reimbursement using SAP Signavio solutions, an illustration of how process intelligence can accelerate transformation in healthcare. Similar quick wins are within reach: streamline patient flow and bed capacity with a single view of clinical and operational signals; optimize pharmacy and inventory with demand forecasting and automated replenishment; strengthen revenue integrity and coding with governed clinical and billing data; provision research datasets with automated de-identification and access controls; and boost workforce productivity through embedded analytics and assistants such as SAP Joule.

A call to action: A phased path to your digital health platform

The destination is not a single product: It is an operating model which supports ongoing transformations. A practical path begins with alignment on priorities: map your landscape, pain points, and the three to five cross-functional use cases that span clinical and operational domains. Define your guiding principles and develop your target modular enterprise architecture accordingly. Start with the data fabric by setting up SAP Business Data Cloud and publishing your first data products, such as Patient 360 and supply-chain performance, with clear governance and policies.  In parallel, you can modernize the core - either by starting fresh with the GROW with SAP journey, designed to consume the entire SAP Business Suite from the public cloud with best practices and continuous innovation, and rapid time‑to‑value or by modernizing your existing landscape with the RISE with SAP journey, typically moving to SAP Cloud ERP in a private‑cloud model combined with specialized public‑cloud LoB solutions. This hybrid path supports a clean‑core roadmap, harmonized processes, and a transformation pace aligned to your needs. Integrate for interoperability by deploying SAP Integration Suite and SAP Health Data Services for FHIR and connecting AnyEMR/AnyRCM and priority partner solutions from SAP partner ecosystem for healthcare industry. Prove value quickly and already during your transformation by launching two time-boxed pilots: one operational, one clinical-adjacent with measurable KPIs and executive sponsorship. Then scale responsibly: expand analytics and AI with governance-by-design and deploy in line with residency and sovereignty requirements.

This is how we aim to help so that your healthcare organizations run better and deliver high-quality, outcomes-based care together with our partners.

entry.title: Bank Statement Reconciliation
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I am performing the bank reconciliation of a vendor payment that had already been posted in the system, using the “Manage Bank Statements” app. When I reconcile it with the corresponding line in the bank statement, I notice that the clearing account shows an open item both in positive and negative amounts.

I also expected that, when reconciling the payment with the bank statement line, the item in the bank clearing account would be automatically cleared, but this is not happening. I would like to understand why this behavior occurs.

My specific questions are:

  • Why does the item in the clearing account not get automatically cleared when the payment is matched with the bank statement?

  • Is it normal for the clearing account to generate two open items (one debit and one credit) after reconciling a payment that was already posted?

  • Should the automatic clearing of the payment with the invoice be executed before or after the bank reconciliation?

  • Does automatic clearing always need to be performed, even when bank reconciliation is done in Bank Statement Processing?

I would appreciate any guidance or experience regarding the correct process flow.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Re: Outbound iDocs are not randomly not generated
entry.content:
1. Analyze the Failed Cases
Since replication isn’t possible, focus on the six historical incidents. Compare the warehouse tasks that did generate iDocs vs. those that did not. Look for subtle differences for storage type, bin type, handling unit assignment, batch status, or special stock indicators. Check if the materials involved had any unusual master data settings (e.g., missing output determination key fields).

2. System Logs & Monitoring

  • SM58 (Transactional RFC): See if any outbound LUWs were stuck or failed at the time.
  • SMQ1/SMQ2 (Queues): Check if the outbound queue had errors.
  • WE02/WE05 (iDoc monitoring): Confirm whether the iDoc was never created or created but failed to send.
  • SLG1 (Application logs): Look for error messages tied to warehouse task confirmation.

3. Potential Root Causes

  • Race conditions: If multiple tasks are confirmed simultaneously, the output determination might skip one.
  • Custom code: If there’s a user exit/BAdI filtering which tasks should trigger iDocs, it may be excluding certain cases unintentionally.
  • Authorization issues: Rarely, if the user confirming the task lacks authorization for the output type, the iDoc won’t be generated.
  • Update termination: If the update task failed silently, the material movement would post but the iDoc creation routine wouldn’t complete.

4. Preventive Measures
Implement additional logging in the iDoc creation user exit/BAdI to capture why an iDoc wasn’t generated. Set up alerts for when a warehouse task is confirmed without a corresponding iDoc (cross‑check tables). If possible, create a reconciliation report comparing SAP bin status vs. MES bin status daily to catch discrepancies early.

entry.title: Re: Qualification in Routing Operations S4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Good day @kylianswinkels98 

Thank you for your question.

In Public Cloud, there is no CBC configuration that lets you maintain a free “list of qualifications” for CA0x routing directly, which is why you cannot find it in CBC or the workforce apps. In the CA01/CA02/CA03, the fields Job/Position/Qualification come from the classic “personnel qualification requirement” concept. In practice, these fields only become fully usable when the system has an underlying qualification catalog and the related integration, which is traditionally HCM or a PEO based qualification setup.

If your tenant uses PEO and supports qualification handling, you can handle qualification via Manage Object Qualification Assignments. This is where qualification is used in the manufacturing context, instead of maintaining a custom list in CA0x.  

Suggested best practice is:

  • Not to create a ZZ field just to replicate GL account or qualification style logic unless you have a clear extensibility requirement.
  • For specific case, the clean approach is:
    • For you to use standard qualification assignment apps, or
    • If not available, do not rely on the qualification fields in CA01/CA02/CA03 because you cannot effectively maintain them in standard Public Cloud.

If your PEO apps exist but CA0x value help is not consistent, i suggest you raise an incident because it may be a UI or scope issue. Else, it is a feature gap which you can submit a improvement request rather than a CBC confit switch.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: What's New in Supply Chain in SAP Cloud ERP 2602?
entry.content:
In this blog, we're excited to introduce you to some of the most impactful updates for Supply Chain in the Cloud ERP 2602 release. This update is all about empowering you with greater visibility, streamlined processes, and enhanced flexibility across your entire supply chain. Whether you work in inventory, warehousing, or transportation, these new features are designed to make your daily work more efficient and intelligent. Below, you'll find a summary of the main highlights from each area of our supply chain solution, and if you follow the links, you'll find a comprehensive overview of further new features in each area from our expert product managers.


2602 Release Highlights Overview

To kick things off, we've put together an overview of our top  supply chain highlights in the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP.

 

Warehouse Management in SAP Cloud ERP 2602

One fresh Warehouse Management feature we're keen to highlight is the new Joule capability to assist with your Warehouse Management Operations, which we'll look at below. Of course, there's much more to come -  make sure to check out @Christian_Ries blog for the full scope of release highlights in Warehouse Management in the 2602 release. 

Joule Enablement For Outbound Delivery Orders & Warehouse Tasks

Building on recent enhancements, we continue to expand the capabilities of Joule, our generative AI assistant. Now, Joule can assist users in accessing and managing key business objects in Warehouse Management using natural language. You can use simple conversational language to process delivery orders and warehouse tasks, ask questions, display data, and receive navigation links to relevant applications. Joule can also perform actions such as creating and updating business objects. This functionality simplifies user interaction and empowers your team to control warehouse operations with greater efficiency and accuracy, directly through a natural language interface.

 

Automatic Packing for Direct Goods Receipt Posting after Production Order Confirmation 

A recent key enhancement automates the packing of goods following production. Previously, a direct goods receipt could be posted automatically upon confirmation of a production order. Now, this process has been enhanced to include automatic packing. The system can be configured to automatically pack any unpacked products into handling units based on predefined packaging instructions. This action is triggered during the confirmation of a manufacturing order, eliminating the need for manual user input. This feature increases efficiency on the production floor by combining automatable steps and improves accuracy by providing total automation from confirmation to packed stock, which reduces the potential for human error.

 

Transportation Management in SAP Cloud ERP 2602

In the below demo, @Markus_Oertelt talks you through the complete transportation lifecycle associated with subcontracting purchase orders. This seamless integration is released in 2602 and enables transportation planning, execution and freight settlement via subcontractors with minimal manual effort and reduced scope for errors. Of course, that's not all, and for an overview of further TM highlights in this release, check this blog out from TM Expert @thomas_roesch.

 

Enable Subcontracting Purchase Order Integration With Transportation Management

This powerful new feature enables seamless integration of subcontracting purchase orders with Transportation Management (TM). Users can now manage the complete transportation lifecycle associated with the subcontracting process, from planning transportation demand based on purchase order requirements to executing movements and settling freight costs for both outbound and inbound processes. For outbound processes, transportation demand from the delivery to the subcontractor can be planned, executed, and settled. For inbound processes, the same applies to the delivery of the finished product. This integration streamlines the end-to-end process, reduces manual effort, ensures accurate cost allocation, and provides real-time visibility, allowing for greater control and transparency over subcontracting logistics.

 


Core Logistics in SAP Cloud ERP 2602

Under core logistics, we include the backbone of any well-oiled supply chain solution, featuring inventory management, delivery management, handling-unit & serial number management. With release 2602, we're particularly excited by the improved user experience when managing physical inventory delivered by the new app Manage Physical Inventory Items List. Of course, that's not all, and for some more core logistics highlights, look no further than the overview blog by @Greg_Hutcheon1. 

Introducing Manage Physical Inventory Item List (App F8634)


In the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP, SAP introduces the new Fiori app Manage Physical Inventory Item List (F8634) to help inventory managers process physical inventory faster and with greater accuracy. Instead of switching between multiple documents and apps, users get a single, structured list report with clear status visibility across all relevant physical inventory items, making it easy to see what’s pending, in progress, or complete.

The app offers flexible filters (for example by plant, storage location, planned count date, and processing status) so managers can quickly focus on the right subset of items—such as “Counted” entries ready for follow-up. From the same screen, they can execute key actions like posting, triggering recounts, or printing, including mass processing across multiple inventory documents. In a typical flow, managers review counted items, identify differences, post items with no variance in one step, and initiate recounts for major discrepancies by setting a recount date and generating a new physical inventory document. If configured, the app also supports compliant processing by prompting for reason codes during posting. Overall, F8634 improves transparency, reduces errors, and streamlines inventory processing from count to resolution.

 


Order Promising

New BAdI for Confirmation Acceptance Rule

In this release, SAP Cloud ERP added a powerful new extension point for Order Promising: the Confirmation Adjustment (Confirmation Acceptance) BAdI, also known as “adjust ATP confirmation.” It lets you take the standard confirmations calculated by SAP ATP—quantities and dates—then apply your own business logic on top after the basic ATP check.

With this BAdI, you can override confirmed quantities or shift confirmation dates to better match business priorities (like customer tiers, allocation rules, or operational constraints). The adjusted confirmation can be written back and persisted in the relevant tables, so downstream processes work with the business-approved result—not just the original ATP output.

To keep things safe and explainable, SAP includes validations to prevent over-confirmation, and the system records the custom outcome in the ATP check log for full transparency. The adjusted values can also influence BOP decisions(for example via FIXMG), giving you more control over how confirmations behave during backorder processing.

Supply Protection Indicator in the Display Product Availability App

The Display Product Availability app now includes an easy-to-understand Supply Protection (SUP) indicator with two states (on/off). It adds immediate context to the available quantity by showing whether that quantity may be subject to supply protection.

Note: The icon is shown as active when supply protection is enabled for the specific product in the specific plant.

A SUP icon now appears directly next to a product’s available quantity. It indicates whether Supply Protection is considered in the availability calculation, helping users quickly interpret what they’re seeing without extra checks. This improves day-to-day decision-making by giving users instant visibility into whether the displayed quantity is “straight availability” or potentially restricted by supply protection—saving time and reducing confusion during availability checks.

 

Summary

This blog provides an overview of the key features and functions delivered in the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP. Make sure to follow the links above to find about more supply chain features in depth with the relevant specialist blogs. Beyond supply chain capabilities, the 2602 SAP Cloud ERP release was packed full of great new features designed to drive success. For an overview of the key highlights from this release, make sure to check out the 2602 release blog by @BeSchulze  

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Click on the enablement wheel below and check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you:

entry.title: Re: A class already exists with the name "LCL_CUSTOM_HEADER"
entry.content:
EDIT 2026-02-20: I'm copying this from the only comment (in 2024), assuming that it has solved the issue.

It's possible that you have moved your local class from one include to another, and you are activating only one include. Activate all the includes at the same time. It's also possible that you have 2 INCLUDE statements of the same include program, or two declarations CLASS lcl_custom_header, in that case fix your code.

entry.title: Re: onSelect script: How to read dimension property value...
entry.content: Amazing it is working as you suggested!! I used getResultMember() instead of getResultSet() — I was trying to get a single member, but getResultSet() is the correct way to get the full row data including properties. I tried to access properties directly as member.properties["ExtLink"] — The correct path is deeper: member[0]["Story"].properties["Story.ExtLink"]. I missed both the array index [0], the dimension name ["Story"], and the fact that the property key needs the dimension prefix "Story.ExtLink" not just "ExtLink".

entry.title: Re: IC Stock Transfer Returns Process w/ outbound and inbound delivery (Private Cloud / On Prem)
entry.content:
Hi @kulsar_007 
you can activate NB for the return as well, but maybe you have different output requirements than for regular PO type. Just food for though.

regarding the other question, I follow here the standard setup (item categories):
the returning site creates a outbound delivery with item category NLLR and movement type 161.
When this is posted goods issue, the stock is in transit and with output type SPED an inbound delivery with item category ELCR and movement type 673 is created. So far, the stock is not received. Only after goods receipt of the inbound delivery, the stock is available.
